title: Recognizing the 125th anniversary of organized Okinawan immigration to the United States.
plain_summary: This bill celebrates 125 years since people from Okinawa first came to the United States in an organized way, starting on January 8, 1900. It honors the many ways Okinawan Americans have helped make America better through their work in government, business, the military, and arts and culture. The bill asks all Americans to recognize this important anniversary by holding special programs and activities to celebrate Okinawan Americans and their contributions to our country.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the one hundred twenty-fifth anniversary of organized Okinawan immigration to the United States.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: On January eighth, nineteen hundred, twenty-six contract workers from the island of Okinawa arrived in Honolulu, Hawaii, on a ship called the S.S. City of China. They were led by Mr. Kyuzo Toyama. This was the first organized group of Okinawan people to come to the United States. The first single Okinawan person to arrive in the United States was Mr. Keizo Kawatsu, who came in eighteen eighty-nine through Vancouver, Canada, then traveled through Los Angeles, California, before settling in Phoenix, Arizona. Hawaii was a major destination for organized Okinawan migration. This movement of people has since spread across North America, South America, and beyond. Today, there are an estimated one hundred thousand or more people of Okinawan ancestry living in the United States. About half of them live in the State of Hawaii. The Okinawan community has made significant contributions to government, business, the military, education, and other areas in the United States. During World War Two, American soldiers of Okinawan heritage fought bravely alongside Japanese American second-generation soldiers in special military units including the four hundred forty-second Regimental Combat Team, the one hundredth Infantry Battalion, the five hundred fifty-second Field Artillery Battalion, and the Military Intelligence Service. During the Battle of Okinawa in nineteen forty-five, servicemembers of Okinawan heritage in the Military Intelligence Service provided life-saving translation and interpretation services. They convinced the local population to surrender to United States forces, which allowed humanitarian aid to be distributed to the local population that had suffered greatly during the war. To provide further humanitarian aid to Okinawa after World War Two, Okinawan Americans gathered donations and worked with the United States military to deliver shipments of clothes, medicine, school supplies, and livestock to Okinawa. Okinawan Americans have established community cultural organizations to promote collaboration and diversity across the country. There are over fifty Okinawan Kenjinkai organizations in twenty-six states and the District of Columbia. Okinawan Americans have held top positions in government, business, the military, and education. These include former Hawaii Governor David Ige, who was the first Okinawan American elected Governor of a United States State. General Paul Nakasone served as Commander of United States Cyber Command and Director of the National Security Agency, becoming the first four-star General of Okinawan ancestry. Other notable leaders include Keith Hayashi as Superintendent of Hawaii State Department of Education, David Roberts as Manager of the Los Angeles Dodgers and first Manager of Asian heritage to lead a team to the World Series and to a World Series title, Mark Taira as Chief Executive Officer and President of King's Hawaiian Bakery, Roy Yamaguchi as celebrity chef and restaurateur, Lynne Yoshiko Nakasone as Okinawan dance performer recognized by the National Endowment for the Arts as a National Heritage Fellow in twenty twelve, Jake Shimabukuro as ukulele virtuoso and composer, and Tamilyn Tomita as actress. Okinawan Americans have kept their unique cultural identity, history, and indigenous languages alive. These languages are recognized by the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization. Okinawa is the birthplace of karate. Okinawan immigrants introduced and spread this martial art and its distinct styles, including Shorin-Ryu, Goju-Ryu, Uechi-Ryu, and Shito-Ryu, to Hawaii, the United States, and the world. The year twenty twenty-five also marks the anniversary of many sister-state and sister-city relationships between Okinawa and the United States. These include the fortieth anniversary of the Hawaii-Okinawa Sister State Relationship, the sixty-fifth anniversary of the Honolulu-Naha Sister City Relationship, the sixty-second anniversary of the Kauai County-Ishigaki Sister City Relationship, the sixtieth anniversary of the Maui County-Miyakojima Sister City Relationship, the thirty-ninth anniversary of the Hawaii County-Nago Sister City Relationship, the twenty-third anniversary of the Lakewood, Washington-Okinawa City Sister City Relationship, the fourteenth anniversary of the Hawaii County-Kumejima Sister City Relationship, the twelfth anniversary of the Redondo Beach, California-Itoman City Sister City Relationship, and the fifth anniversary of the Honolulu-Kin Friendship Agreement. Okinawan Americans will continue to keep and share their unique cultural identity and heritage and build deeper relationships between the peoples of Okinawa and the United States.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Resolves
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes the one hundred twenty-fifth anniversary of Okinawan immigration to the United States and the historic contributions of Okinawan Americans to the Nation. The House reaffirms the unique cultural and people-to-people ties between the United States and Okinawa. The House urges the people of the United States to honor the one hundred twenty-fifth anniversary of Okinawan immigration to the United States with appropriate programs and activities.
